questions.question: Elvis Presley died on August 16, 1977, at age 42. At which of his homes was he found?
questions.explanation: Elvis Presley was found unresponsive at his Graceland estate in Memphis, Tennessee, on August 16, 1977. He died of cardiac arrest, exacerbated by years of prescription drug use. Graceland is now one of the most visited private homes in the United States, open to tours year-round. His death sent shockwaves through popular culture — he was only 42 but had been the most famous entertainer in the world.
questions.memory_hook: Elvis died at Graceland, August 16, 1977. Age 42. Memphis, Tennessee. Graceland now gets 600,000 visitors a year. He was so famous that when he died, his weight had doubled and he was barely performing — yet his death was world news and has stayed in cultural consciousness ever since.

questions.question: The Crusades, a series of religious wars, were launched to recapture which city from Muslim control?
questions.explanation: The Crusades (1095-1291) were a series of religious wars called by the Catholic Church to recapture Jerusalem and the Holy Land from Muslim rule. Pope Urban II called the First Crusade in 1095. Christian crusaders captured Jerusalem in 1099. The city changed hands multiple times over the following centuries. The Crusades had lasting effects on relations between Christianity and Islam.
questions.memory_hook: Crusades = recapture Jerusalem. Pope Urban II launched them in 1095. Christians took Jerusalem in 1099, killing most of the population. Saladin retook it in 1187, allowing peaceful coexistence. The Third Crusade (Richard the Lionheart vs Saladin) never retook the city. They negotiated instead.

questions.question: The Wright Brothers made their historic first flight at Kitty Hawk. In which US state is Kitty Hawk located?
questions.explanation: Kitty Hawk and Kill Devil Hills are located in the Outer Banks of North Carolina. The Wright Brothers chose the location because of its strong, consistent winds, soft sandy landing surfaces, and remoteness (few witnesses if things went wrong). North Carolina's state motto is 'First in Flight' and the first flight appears on the state's license plates.
questions.memory_hook: Kitty Hawk = North Carolina. 'First in Flight' is on NC's license plates. The Wright Brothers chose it for the reliable Atlantic winds and the soft sand for landing. The nearby Kill Devil Hills is actually where the flights happened — but Kitty Hawk was the nearest post office for the famous telegram home.

questions.question: In which sport would you execute a 'spike' to score a point?
questions.explanation: A spike (also called an attack or kill) in volleyball is an offensive shot where a player jumps and powerfully hits the ball downward into the opponent's court. It is the primary way to score points from the front row. A well-executed spike can travel at over 80 mph, giving the defenders very little time to react. The libero (defensive specialist) cannot spike the ball from the front zone.
questions.memory_hook: Spike = volleyball's main scoring attack. Jump, hit down, score. Can exceed 80 mph — faster than most tennis serves. The libero (defensive specialist wearing a different color jersey) specifically cannot do this — their job is to receive and defend.

questions.question: Which sport uses the term 'offside' as a way to prevent attacking players from gaining an unfair advantage?
questions.explanation: The offside rule exists in both soccer and ice hockey. In soccer, a player is offside if they're closer to the opponent's goal line than the ball and the second-last defender at the moment the ball is played to them. In ice hockey, a player can't enter the attacking zone before the puck. The rule is designed to prevent players from 'cherry picking' by stationing themselves near the goal.
questions.memory_hook: Offside = soccer and ice hockey. Prevent cherry picking near the opponent's goal. In soccer it's incredibly complex and now checked by VAR technology. In hockey it's simpler — the puck must enter the zone before the player. Both sports reward buildup play over just camping near the goal.

questions.question: The film 'Schindler's List' was based on a book called 'Schindler's Ark.' Who wrote the novel?
questions.explanation: Thomas Keneally, an Australian author, wrote Schindler's Ark in 1982, which won the Booker Prize. The book was renamed Schindler's List for the US market and later for Spielberg's 1993 film adaptation. Keneally researched the book after meeting Leopold Pfefferberg, one of the actual Schindlerjuden (Schindler's Jews), in a Los Angeles luggage shop.
questions.memory_hook: Schindler's Ark = Thomas Keneally (Australian). Won the Booker Prize. Keneally met one of Schindler's actual survivors in a luggage shop — the survivor spent the rest of his life trying to get the story told. Spielberg's film finally did it to a global audience.

questions.question: Born Christopher Wallace in Brooklyn, this rapper released 'Ready to Die' in 1994 and 'Life After Death' in 1997, days before he was shot and killed in Los Angeles. By what name is he best known?
questions.explanation: The Notorious B.I.G. (also known as Biggie Smalls) is widely regarded as one of the greatest rappers of all time. His debut album 'Ready to Die' is considered a classic of East Coast hip-hop. He was murdered in a drive-by shooting in Los Angeles on March 9, 1997, at age 24.
questions.memory_hook: Christopher Wallace = Biggie = The Notorious B.I.G. Brooklyn born, murdered 1997.

questions.question: The animated film 'Spirited Away' (2001), which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, was directed by which Japanese filmmaker?
questions.explanation: Hayao Miyazaki directed Spirited Away for Studio Ghibli in 2001. It became the highest-grossing film in Japanese history and won the Golden Bear at the Berlin International Film Festival as well as the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature (2003) — the first non-English animated film to win the Oscar. Miyazaki created it for his daughter and her friends, imagining what 10-year-old girls might enjoy.
questions.memory_hook: Spirited Away = Miyazaki, 2001. First and still the only non-English animated film to win the Oscar for Best Animated Feature. The highest-grossing film in Japanese history when released. Miyazaki made it specifically for children, not adults — which is why adults are so moved by it.

questions.question: Which country contains the source of the Nile River, near Lake Victoria?
questions.explanation: The White Nile — the longer branch of the Nile — originates near Lake Victoria, with Jinja, Uganda, considered the principal source. The Blue Nile (which carries more water and sediment) begins in Ethiopia at Lake Tana. The two branches meet in Khartoum, Sudan. The search for the Nile's source was one of the great geographical quests of the 19th century, involving explorers like Speke, Burton, and Livingstone.
questions.memory_hook: Nile source = near Lake Victoria, Uganda (White Nile). Ethiopia supplies the Blue Nile, which carries most of the water and all of the famous silt that made Egypt fertile. Finding the Nile's source was the 19th century's equivalent of a moon landing — explorers died trying.

questions.question: What is the name of the phenomenon where a full moon appears larger and brighter when it is closest to Earth in its orbit?
questions.explanation: A supermoon occurs when a full moon coincides with the Moon's closest approach to Earth (perigee) in its elliptical orbit. The Moon can appear up to 14% larger and 30% brighter than when it is at its farthest point (apogee). The term 'supermoon' was coined by astrologer Richard Nolle in 1979 and popularized by media in the 2010s. Astronomers prefer the term 'perigee full moon.'
questions.memory_hook: Supermoon = full moon at its closest point to Earth. Up to 14% bigger, 30% brighter. The Moon's orbit is elliptical, not circular — so its distance varies. Astronomers roll their eyes at the term because the size difference is subtle. Photographers and poets love it.

questions.question: The Murray River, Australia's longest at 2,508 km, forms the border between which two Australian states?
questions.explanation: The Murray River forms the boundary between Victoria (south bank) and New South Wales (north bank) for most of its length before flowing through South Australia to the sea. Together with the Darling River, the Murray-Darling Basin is Australia's most important agricultural region, producing roughly 40% of the country's food. The river suffers from over-allocation of water for irrigation, causing environmental damage. Murray cod — a large freshwater fish — are native to the river.
questions.memory_hook: Murray River: 2,508 km, forms Victoria/NSW border. Murray-Darling Basin produces 40% of Australia's food. Severely stressed from over-irrigation. Murray cod can live 50+ years. The Murray's mouth now barely reaches the sea in drought years — a classic river over-allocation story.

questions.question: What environmental phenomenon occurs when excess nutrients (especially nitrogen and phosphorus from fertilizer runoff) cause rapid algae growth in water bodies, depleting oxygen and killing aquatic life?
questions.explanation: Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients — mainly nitrates and phosphates from agricultural fertilizers, sewage, and detergents — wash into rivers, lakes, or coastal areas. This triggers explosive algae and cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) blooms. When the blooms die, bacteria decompose them, consuming oxygen and creating 'dead zones' where fish cannot survive. Gulf of Mexico dead zones (caused by Mississippi River runoff from Midwestern farms) and Lake Erie algae blooms are major examples.
questions.memory_hook: Eutrophication: fertilizer runoff → algae bloom → algae dies → bacteria decompose it → oxygen depleted → fish die. Gulf of Mexico dead zone (from Mississippi River farm runoff) is the size of New Jersey. Lake Erie turned green in 2014. A predictable consequence of industrial agriculture without proper runoff management.
